title: The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism
subtitle: Die protestantische Ethik und der 'Geist' des Kapitalismus
author: Max Weber

recommendedTranslation.title: The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism (Scribner's Edition)
recommendedTranslation.note: Standard scholarly translation with introduction. This 2011 Oxford edition is the primary reference for this guide.

alternativeTranslations.title: The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism (Comparative Re-edition)
alternativeTranslations.note: Recommended alternative with comparative orientation, shorter introduction, and useful appendices.

alternativeTranslations.translator: Peter Baehr and Gordon C. Wells
alternativeTranslations.title: The Protestant Ethic and the 'Spirit' of Capitalism and Other Writings
alternativeTranslations.note: Includes broader selection of Weber's related writings; excellent for extended study.

alternativeTranslations.title: The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism (with Translator's Introduction)
alternativeTranslations.note: Giddens's introduction provides important contextualization of Weber's argument within sociological theory.

rationale: Selected as Book #2 for sociology because it is one of the four or five most important sociological texts ever written—a founding document of economic sociology whose argument about the cultural origins of capitalismentereds standard debates in social theory, economic history, political theory, and comparative religion. Its thesis about an elective affinity between ascetic Protestantism and modern capitalism is both historically specific (Northern Europe, 16th–19th centuries) and theoretically generative across all discussions of culture-economy relationships.
excerpt: Max Weber's foundational 1905 essay investigating how the religious values of ascetic Protestantism—above all Calvinist predestination and the concept of a worldly calling—helped create the cultural conditions for the emergence of modern rational capitalism. Weber's thesis of an 'elective affinity' between Protestant ethic and capitalist spirit remains one of the most debated arguments in the history of social thought, spawning over a century of scholarship across sociology, economic history, political theory, and the comparative study of religion.
